title: Brute-Force Login Detection

compliance.assurance_rationale: HITRUST CSF v11 01.b User access management — detecting brute-force credential attacks evidences the authentication-control portion of user access management required for HITRUST certification.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of HITRUST 01.b (User access management) — Splunk UC-9.1.1: Brute-Force Login Detection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-9.1.1' running on Windows Security Event Log (Event ID 4625 — failed logon), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CJIS v5.9.4 5.5.1 Access control — identification — the UC fires on repeated authentication failures against CJI-access accounts, the detection signal CJIS relies on to flag credential compromise.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of CJIS 5.5.1 (Access control - identification) — Splunk UC-9.1.1: Brute-Force Login Detection.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-9.1.1' running on Windows Security Event Log (Event ID 4625 — failed logon), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: UK Cyber Essentials Montpellier CE.SAU.1 Secure authentication & access — brute-force detection is the primary monitoring control listed under the authentication requirement.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of Cyber Essentials CE.SAU.1 (Secure authentication & access) — Splunk UC-9.1.1: Brute-Force Login Detection.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PSD2 Art.97 Strong customer authentication — brute-force failure rates are one of the transaction-risk signals used to back SCA-invocation decisions.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of PSD2 Art.97 (Strong customer authentication) — Splunk UC-9.1.1: Brute-Force Login Detection.

dataSources: Windows Security Event Log (Event ID 4625 — failed logon)
spl:
index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode=4625
| stats count by Account_Name, Source_Network_Address
| where count > 10
| sort -count
description: Brute-force attacks are a primary credential compromise vector. Early detection prevents account takeover.
value: Brute-force attacks are a primary credential compromise vector. Early detection prevents account takeover.
implementation: Deploy the Universal Forwarder on all Domain Controllers with `[WinEventLog://Security]` enabled. Ensure the GPO enables Audit Logon Success and Failure. Alert with `stats count by Account_Name, src span=15m` when count exceeds 10. Suppress break-glass and service accounts via a `privileged_accounts` lookup to reduce false positives.
visualization: Table (accounts with failure counts), Line chart (failure rate over time), Geo map (source IPs).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  where Authentication.action="failure"
  by Authentication.user,Authentication.src span=1h
| where count > 10
knownFalsePositives: Service accounts with rotated passwords, scripts using old credentials, monitoring tools polling without auth refresh, shared kiosks, or legitimate lockouts after user mistakes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_windows`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Log (Event ID 4625 — failed logon).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Deploy the Universal Forwarder on all Domain Controllers with `[WinEventLog://Security]` enabled. Ensure the GPO enables Audit Logon Success and Failure. Alert with `stats count by Account_Name, src span=15m` when count exceeds 10. Suppress break-glass and service accounts via a `privileged_accounts` lookup to reduce false positives.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode=4625
| stats count by Account_Name, Source_Network_Address
| where count > 10
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Brute-Force Login Detection** — Brute-force attacks are a primary credential compromise vector. Early detection prevents account takeover.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Log (Event ID 4625 — failed logon). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_windows`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: wineventlog; **sourcetype**: WinEventLog:Security. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=wineventlog, sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by Account_Name, Source_Network_Address** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where count > 10` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  where Authentication.action="failure"
  by Authentication.user Authentication.src span=1h
| where count > 10
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Brute-Force Login Detection** — Brute-force attacks are a primary credential compromise vector. Early detection prevents account takeover.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Log (Event ID 4625 — failed logon). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_windows`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Filters the current rows with `where count > 10` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with Event Viewer on domain controllers (or exported Security logs) and with Active Directory Users and Computers for the same objects and time window.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (accounts with failure counts), Line chart (failure rate over time), Geo map (source IPs).
grandmaExplanation: We watch for many failed sign-in attempts from the same place or against the same account so we can stop password guessing before a takeover.
dataModelAcceleration: Enable Data Model Acceleration for the CIM `Authentication.Authentication` dataset once identity and sign-in data are CIM-tagged; otherwise `cimSpl` tstats may return no rows.
